secondary_citations: {"Akormedi et al. 2013 (Environmental Research)", "Amoyaw-Osei et al. 2011 (GreenAd Ltd)"}
notes: Agbogbloshie site in Accra documented as one of the world's largest informal e-waste processing sites. Open burning and acid leaching documented. Significant health impacts on workers.
Lagos (Alaba International Market) is a major informal e-waste hub. Documented in multiple BAN (Basel Action Network) investigations. Large informal sector processes exported used electronics.

primary_citation: Naqvi et al. 2014 (Resources Conservation and Recycling)
notes: Karachi and Lahore have significant informal e-waste processing. Acid leaching of printed circuit boards documented.
Dhaka has documented informal e-waste workshops. Processing of used electronics from EU and North America documented.

Guiyu (Guangdong) was historically the world's largest e-waste processing site. Chinese import bans (2018 National Sword policy) have significantly reduced formal imports but informal processing continues. Downgraded from major_hub to documented to reflect policy change.

primary_citation: Sinha-Khetriwal et al. 2005 (Resources Conservation and Recycling)
notes: Multiple cities (Delhi, Mumbai, Bangalore, Chennai) have documented informal e-waste dismantling. India's E-Waste Management Rules (2016, amended 2022) have increased formal sector capacity but informal sector remains dominant.
Dakar has documented informal e-waste processing, partly from EU exports.
Dar es Salaam has growing informal e-waste sector. Data limited.
Kampala has documented informal e-waste workshops.

primary_citation: El-Haggar 2007 (Sustainable Industrial Design and Waste Management)
notes: Cairo has documented informal e-waste recycling. Egypt is a transit country for e-waste from EU to sub-Saharan Africa.
Java has documented informal e-waste processing. Growing formal sector.
Metro Manila has documented informal e-waste recycling. The Philippines has ratified the Basel Convention.

primary_citation: Rahman et al. 2014 (E3S Web of Conferences)
notes: Malaysia is a known transit and processing country for e-waste from Australia and Japan. Illegal imports documented by authorities.
Border cities (Tijuana, Ciudad Juárez) have documented informal e-waste recycling, partly linked to maquiladora industry.
Brazil has significant informal e-waste sector despite being a major generator. Growing formal sector.
